(October 16, 1925 – November 22, 1983) was an American actor, perhaps best known for his portrayal of veteran cop Sgt. Phil Esterhaus on *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")*—a role for which he won two [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") for [Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Supporting_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series#1980s "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series") in [1981](/wiki/33rd_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "33rd Primetime Emmy Awards") and [1982](/wiki/34th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "34th Primetime Emmy Awards"). Among his other credits were the short-lived television series *[Delvecchio](/wiki/Delvecchio_(TV_series) "Delvecchio (TV series)")* (1976–1977); and the films *[The War Lord](/wiki/The_War_Lord "The War Lord")* (1965), *[They Shoot Horses, Don't They?](/wiki/They_Shoot_Horses,_Don%27t_They%3F_(film) "They Shoot Horses, Don't They? (film)")* (1969), *[The Todd Killings](/wiki/The_Todd_Killings "The Todd Killings")* (1971), and *[The Longest Yard](/wiki/The_Longest_Yard_(1974_film) "The Longest Yard (1974 film)")* (1974).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor (1925–1983)

For other people named Michael Conrad, see [Michael Conrad (disambiguation)](/wiki/Michael_Conrad_(disambiguation) "Michael Conrad (disambiguation)").

| Michael Conrad | |
| --- | --- |
| Conrad in *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")*, 1981 | |
| Born | (1925-10-16)October 16, 1925  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.[[1]](#cite_note-nytimes-1) |
| Died | November 22, 1983(1983-11-22) (aged 58)  [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, U.S. |
| Occupation | Actor |
| Years active | 1937–1983 |
| Spouses | * Denise McCluggage  ​  ​ (m. 1953; div. 1954)​ * Emilie Demille  ​  ​ (m. 1963; div. 1967)​ * Sima Goldberg  ​ (m. 1974)​ |

**Michael Conrad** (October 16, 1925 – November 22, 1983) was an American actor, perhaps best known for his portrayal of veteran cop Sgt. Phil Esterhaus on *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")*—a role for which he won two [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") for [Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Supporting_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series#1980s "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series") in [1981](/wiki/33rd_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "33rd Primetime Emmy Awards") and [1982](/wiki/34th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "34th Primetime Emmy Awards"). Among his other credits were the short-lived television series *[Delvecchio](/wiki/Delvecchio_(TV_series) "Delvecchio (TV series)")* (1976–1977); and the films *[The War Lord](/wiki/The_War_Lord "The War Lord")* (1965), *[They Shoot Horses, Don't They?](/wiki/They_Shoot_Horses,_Don%27t_They%3F_(film) "They Shoot Horses, Don't They? (film)")* (1969), *[The Todd Killings](/wiki/The_Todd_Killings "The Todd Killings")* (1971), and *[The Longest Yard](/wiki/The_Longest_Yard_(1974_film) "The Longest Yard (1974 film)")* (1974).

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Conrad&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

Conrad served in the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). He had a long acting career in television from the 1950s to the 1980s. In 1962 he appeared in the television series *[Car 54, Where Are You?](/wiki/Car_54,_Where_Are_You%3F "Car 54, Where Are You?")* in an uncredited part as a construction worker. He played Felton Grimes, the title character and murder victim, in the 1963 *[Perry Mason](/wiki/Perry_Mason_(1957_TV_series) "Perry Mason (1957 TV series)")* episode "The Case of the Bigamous Spouse", and in 1965 played the role of a villain named AC in *[My Favorite Martian](/wiki/My_Favorite_Martian "My Favorite Martian")*, "Martin's Revoltin' Development", and played the role of Paul in *[The F.B.I.](/wiki/The_F.B.I._(TV_series) "The F.B.I. (TV series)")* (season 1, episode 24), "The Man Who Went Mad by Mistake". He also had a notable supporting part in the [1969 film](/wiki/1969_film "1969 film") *[They Shoot Horses, Don't They?](/wiki/They_Shoot_Horses,_Don%27t_They%3F_(film) "They Shoot Horses, Don't They? (film)")*, about a depression-era dance marathon.

In 1972, Conrad played [Michael Stivic](/wiki/Michael_Stivic "Michael Stivic")'s conventional Polish-American Uncle Casimir on two episodes of *[All in the Family](/wiki/All_in_the_Family "All in the Family")*.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The same year, he appeared, together with [Richard Crenna](/wiki/Richard_Crenna "Richard Crenna") and [Alain Delon](/wiki/Alain_Delon "Alain Delon"), in the French-language film *[Un flic](/wiki/Un_flic "Un flic")*, directed by [Jean-Pierre Melville](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Melville "Jean-Pierre Melville"). He also had a memorable role in the [1974 film](/wiki/1974_film "1974 film") *[The Longest Yard](/wiki/The_Longest_Yard_(1974_film) "The Longest Yard (1974 film)")*, playing Nate Scarboro, a retired NFL tight end ([New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants")) who was also the head coach for "the Mean Machine", the team of prisoners put together by [Burt Reynolds](/wiki/Burt_Reynolds "Burt Reynolds")'s character Paul Crewe to play the team of guards.
During the 1976–77 season of *[Delvecchio](/wiki/Delvecchio_(TV_series) "Delvecchio (TV series)")*, Conrad was a regular as Lt. Macavan.

Other notable films in which Conrad appeared throughout his career include *[Requiem for a Heavyweight](/wiki/Requiem_for_a_Heavyweight_(film) "Requiem for a Heavyweight (film)")* (1962), *[The War Lord](/wiki/The_War_Lord "The War Lord")* (1965), *[Blackbeard's Ghost](/wiki/Blackbeard%27s_Ghost "Blackbeard's Ghost")* (1968), *[Castle Keep](/wiki/Castle_Keep "Castle Keep")* (1969), *[Monte Walsh](/wiki/Monte_Walsh_(1970_film) "Monte Walsh (1970 film)")* (1970), *[The Todd Killings](/wiki/The_Todd_Killings "The Todd Killings")* (1971), *[Thumb Tripping](/wiki/Thumb_Tripping "Thumb Tripping")* (1972), *[Scream Blacula Scream](/wiki/Scream_Blacula_Scream "Scream Blacula Scream")* (1973), and *[Harry and Walter Go to New York](/wiki/Harry_and_Walter_Go_to_New_York "Harry and Walter Go to New York")* (1976).

Conrad is perhaps best known for his role as [Sgt. Philip Freemason Esterhaus](/wiki/List_of_Hill_Street_Blues_characters#Sergeant_Philip_"Phil"_Freemason_Esterhaus "List of Hill Street Blues characters") on the 1981–1987 police drama *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")*, a role he played for 71 episodes until his death.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Conrad&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Death")]

Conrad died from [urethral cancer](/wiki/Urethral_cancer "Urethral cancer") in November 1983,[[4]](#cite_note-washpost-4) during the fourth season of *Hill Street Blues*. The show's writers wrote his death into the show, with the cast offering an affectionate tribute to their colleague and friend.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Spouses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Conrad&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Spouses")]

* [Denise McCluggage](/wiki/Denise_McCluggage "Denise McCluggage") (1953–1954; divorced)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Emilie Demille (October 30, 1963 – February 1967; divorced)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Sima Goldberg (January 27, 1974 – November 22, 1983; his death)[[4]](#cite_note-washpost-4)

* *[The Incredible Hulk](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(1978_TV_series) "The Incredible Hulk (1978 TV series)")* (1981, TV Series) as Emerson Fletcher
* *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")* (1981–1984, TV Series) (65 episodes) as Sgt. Phil Esterhaus (final appearance)
